title: Lovecraftesque - Cosmic Horror Storytelling Game
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NJelRHbyeWw

Daniel (@dungeondive) dives deep into Lovecraftesque, a GMless storytelling game that captures cosmic horror's slow-burn dread better than most actual RPGs. This isn't a traditional game with mechanics—there are no dice, no character sheets, no stats. Instead, players collaboratively construct Lovecraftian mysteries using structured prompts while secretly tracking their own supernatural conclusions. The brilliance lies in how it recreates classic cosmic horror pacing: Act One governed by the "creeping horror" rule (nothing overtly supernatural can be directly witnessed) gives way to Act Two where doubt crumbles, then Act Three's accelerated descent into eldritch chaos.

Daniel's setup example perfectly illustrates the magic: a family manor hiding canine-human hybrid experiments, ancient sorcerer-servants, and a demonic dog god—details the protagonists dismiss through reasonable doubt until suddenly they can't. Each scene plays a clue card (anything from "strange architecture" to "bizarre practices"), players react narratively, then secretly jump to supernatural conclusions that might've been right or wrong all along. With six base scenarios plus three expansion packs offering more scenarios, you could generate stories from Roman Pompeii to 15th-century witchcraft. Daniel also notes the game works perfectly as a tool for solo Call of Cthulhu or Karthur Borg—these cards are fuel for improvisation. Thoughtfully organized, beautifully designed, and genuinely thrilling to play.

----

Have you tried a pure storytelling game? Would Lovecraftesque enhance your solo horror RPG sessions, or would you play it for the stories alone?
